lists.title: Xhosa A1 Grammar
lists.description: Foundations: subject concords, the first noun classes, copulatives, negation, locatives, having.

lists.title: Xhosa A2 Grammar
lists.description: Possessive/object/adjective concords, recent past, future, demonstratives.

lists.title: Xhosa B1 Grammar
lists.description: Negation across tenses, subjunctive, remote past, relative clauses.

points.title: Subject concords and the present (ndi-, u-, ba-…)
points.explanation: Every verb starts with a subject concord: ndi- (I), u- (you / he-she cl.1), si- (we), ni- (you all), ba- (they). The long present adds -ya- when nothing follows the verb: Ndiyafunda (I am studying); with an object the -ya- drops: Ndifunda isiXhosa (I study Xhosa).

drills.hint: to learn + 'I' concord (no -ya- before an object)

points.title: Noun classes 1/2: um-/aba- (people)
points.function: Talk about people, one or many
points.explanation: Xhosa nouns carry class prefixes. Class 1/2 is people: umntu/abantu (person/people), umfundi/abafundi (student/s), umntwana/abantwana (child/ren). The class drives agreement on everything that follows.
points.culture_note: The class system (about 15 classes) is the engine of the whole language — one class pair at a time is the way in.

drills.hint: class 1a takes oo- in the plural

points.title: Noun classes 5/6 and 7/8
points.function: Use the ili-/ama- and isi-/izi- families
points.explanation: Class 5/6: i(li)-/ama- — ilizwe/amazwe (country/ies), iqanda/amaqanda (egg/s). Class 7/8: isi-/izi- — isitya/izitya (dish/es); isi- also names languages: isiXhosa, isiNgesi (English).

points.title: Noun classes 9/10: in-/izin-
points.explanation: Class 9/10 covers many everyday nouns and animals: inja/izinja (dog/s), indlu/izindlu (house/s), incwadi/iincwadi (book/s — long ii- plural). Their agreements use i-/zi-: Inja iyatya (The dog is eating), Izinja ziyatya.

points.title: Identifying copulatives (ngu-, yi-)
points.explanation: 'It is X' prefixes the noun: ngu- for class 1/1a (people/names) — NguThemba (It's Themba), ngumfundi (he's a student); yi- for class 9 — yincwadi (it's a book); li-, si-, etc. for other classes. Negation prefixes asi-: asingumfundi (he is not a student).

points.title: Present negative (andi-…-i)
points.explanation: Wrap the verb in a negative concord and final -i: ndiyafunda → andifundi (I don't study), uyafuna → akafuni (he doesn't want — u- becomes aka-), bayasebenza → abasebenzi (they don't work).

drills.hint: to speak + negative, I (final vowel changes)

drills.translation: I don't live in Cape Town.

points.title: Locatives (e-…-ini)
points.explanation: Places are made with e- (and often -ini): isikolo → esikolweni (at school), indlu → endlwini (in the house), iKapa → eKapa (in Cape Town). Ndihlala eKapa (I live in Cape Town), Ndiya esikolweni (I'm going to school).

drills.translation: I am going to town tomorrow.
The children play in the park.

points.title: Questions (ntoni, bani, phi)
points.explanation: Question words follow the verb: Ufuna ntoni? (What do you want?), Ngubani igama lakho? (What/who is your name?), Uhlala phi? (Where do you live?). Yes/no questions just use rising intonation, or open with Ingaba: Ingaba uyeza? (Are you coming?).

points.title: To have: -na-
points.explanation: 'Have' fuses -na- with the noun: ndi + na + incwadi → Ndinencwadi (I have a book), Unemali (you have / she has money), Banabantwana (they have children). Negative: Andinamali (I have no money).

points.title: Possessive concords (yam, wam, bam…)
points.explanation: The possessive agrees with the THING possessed: incwadi yam (my book, cl.9 ya-), umntwana wam (my child, cl.1 wa-), isitya sam (my dish, cl.7 sa-), abantwana bam (my children, cl.2 ba-). The endings -m (my), -kho (your), -khe (his/her) stay constant.

points.title: Recent past (-ile)
points.explanation: The recent past ends in -ile when the verb closes the clause: Ndifundile (I studied / have studied), Bafikile (They have arrived). Before an object the short form -e is used: Ndibone umfundi? — commonly Ndibonile in isolation; keep -ile clause-final at this level.

points.title: Future (-za ku-)
points.explanation: The future combines -za (come) with the infinitive: Ndiza kufunda (I will study), Uza kufika ngomso (She will arrive tomorrow), Siza kuthetha (We will talk). Colloquially it contracts (ndizo-/ndizaku-).

drills.translation: I am going to work tomorrow.
We are going to go to Cape Town.
drills.hint: infinitive 'to go' after the future marker

drills.translation: The children are going to play.
What are you going to do tomorrow?

points.title: Object concords
points.function: Point the verb at its object
points.explanation: The object can live inside the verb as a concord before the stem: Ndiyayifunda (incwadi) — I am reading it (the book, cl.9 -yi-); Ndiyambona (umntu) — I see him (cl.1 -m-); Ndiyazithanda (izinja) — I love them (cl.10 -zi-).

drills.translation: The book? I am reading it.
The water? I am drinking it.

points.title: Adjective concords (omhle, entle…)
points.explanation: Adjectives take a concord from the noun's class: umntwana omhle (a beautiful child), abantwana abahle, incwadi entle (a nice book, cl.9), isitya esihle. The stem (-hle beautiful, -khulu big, -ncinci small) is constant; the concord changes.

points.title: Demonstratives (lo, le, eli…)
points.explanation: Demonstratives agree with the class: lo mntu (this person, cl.1), le ncwadi (this book, cl.9), eli qanda (this egg, cl.5), esi sitya (this dish, cl.7), aba bantwana (these children, cl.2). 'That' lengthens: loo, leyo, elo…

points.title: Negative past and future
points.explanation: Negative past uses a-…-anga: Andifundanga (I didn't study), Abafikanga (they didn't arrive). Negative future inserts -zi: Andizi kufunda (I will not study), Akazi kufika (she won't arrive).

drills.translation: Themba is not going to arrive tomorrow.
drills.hint: future negative, he — the short -zi form

drills.translation: We are not going to play tomorrow.
drills.hint: infinitive 'to play' after the negative future

points.title: Subjunctive (-e) and ma- (let's)
points.explanation: The subjunctive swaps final -a for -e: Masifunde! (Let's study!, ma- + si- + -e), Ndifuna ukuba ufunde (I want you to study), Yiza ukuze sithethe (Come so that we talk).

drills.translation: I want you to speak well.
What do you want me to do?

points.title: Remote past (a-…-a)
points.explanation: The remote past fuses the concord with -a-: ndi + a → nda-: Ndahlala eKapa (I lived in Cape Town, back then), Wafunda eRhini (He studied in Makhanda), Bathetha (they spoke, long ago). It is the storytelling past.

drills.translation: I lived in Cape Town long ago.
I worked in Johannesburg long ago.
Mum lived in East London long ago.
We studied in Makhanda long ago.

drills.hint: to grow up + remote past, he

points.title: Relative clauses (o-…-yo)
points.explanation: Relatives wrap the verb in a relative concord and -yo: umntwana ofundayo (the child who studies), abantwana abafundayo, incwadi endiyifundayo (the book that I am reading — with the object concord inside).

drills.translation: the book that I am reading

drills.hint: to love + relative frame with 'I' and a class-15 object

points.title: Passive (-wa)
points.function: Say what is done, without the doer
points.explanation: Insert -w- before the final vowel: funda → fundwa, thetha → thethwa: Incwadi iyafundwa (The book is being read), IsiXhosa sithethwa eKapa (Xhosa is spoken in Cape Town), Umsebenzi wenziwe (The work has been done).

drills.translation: Xhosa is spoken in Cape Town.
The children are loved by their mother.

points.title: Causative (-isa)
points.explanation: -isa turns 'do' into 'cause to do': funda (learn) → fundisa (teach), sebenza (work) → sebenzisa (use — 'make work'): Utitshala ufundisa abantwana (The teacher teaches the children), Ndisebenzisa ifowuni (I use a phone).

drills.translation: Mum is putting the child to sleep.
I am showing the book to the students.
The teachers teach the children Xhosa.

points.title: Applicative (-ela)
points.function: Do something for or to someone
points.explanation: -ela adds a beneficiary or direction: bhala (write) → bhalela (write to/for): Ndibhalela umama ileta (I am writing mother a letter); sebenza → sebenzela (work for): Usebenzela inkampani (She works for a company).

drills.translation: I am writing a letter to mother.
She works for a big company.
Mum cooks food for the children.
I am buying a book for my friend.
He sends money to his mother.
We are singing a song for the baby.

points.title: Reciprocal (-ana)
points.function: Say people do things to each other
points.explanation: -ana makes the action mutual: thanda → thandana (love each other): Bayathandana (They love each other); bona → bonana (see each other): Sobonana ngomso (We'll see each other tomorrow); ncedana (help each other).

drills.translation: See you tomorrow! (we shall see each other)
People greet each other in the morning.

points.title: Conditions with ukuba
points.explanation: Ukuba opens conditions and 'that' clauses: Ukuba uyafunda, uza kuphumelela (If you study, you will pass), Ndiyazi ukuba uyeza (I know that you are coming). 'When(ever)' uses xa with the participial: Xa ndifunda… (When I study…).

drills.translation: If you study, you will pass.
I know that you are coming.
If you want to succeed, study.
I don't know when he is going to arrive.
If it rains, we will stay in the house.
I am glad that you have arrived.
